atoms.text: There is no single secret to shipping software.
What actually works is shrinking the batch: ship a tiny change every day so you learn every day.
Beginners believe you need a big launch for your work to matter — a belief Ada rejects.
atoms.verbatim: you need a big launch to matter

atoms.text: Big launches hide what users actually want behind months of guessing.
Ada would rather be slightly embarrassed by version one than proud of a version that never ships.
atoms.verbatim: I'd rather be slightly embarrassed by version one than proud of a version that never ships

atoms.text: Polishing something nobody uses is procrastination with extra steps.
Ada's rule: ship the smallest thing a real person can react to, then let their reaction tell you what to build next.
The build–react loop, not talent, is what compounds over a career.
Your editor and tool stack are not what make you fast.
The fastest people Ada knows use boring tools they have used for years.
Switching tools every month feels productive but is a tax you pay over and over.
The myth, which Ada rejects, that more tools mean more output.
Every new tool is a new thing to maintain and forget.
Ada keeps one notebook, one editor, and one terminal, and almost never changes them.
Focus is a budget, not a feeling: you spend it on the work or on the meta-work.
atoms.verbatim: Focus is a budget, not a feeling

atoms.text: Meta-work is sneaky because it looks like progress.
The boring stack is the fast stack.
atoms.verbatim: The boring stack is the fast stack

edges.note: the belief vs the method that works
Ada's rebuttal of the big-launch belief
Ada's rebuttal of the more-tools myth
